title: Feature-First Architecture
objectives: Understand feature-first vs layer-first via a real-life analogy
Combine feature-first with Clean Architecture (nested layers)
Recognize when feature-first pays off (and when it doesn't)

sections.markdown:
**Think of two ways to organize a hospital.**

- **Layer-first hospital:** all X-ray machines on Floor 1, all surgery rooms on Floor 2, all recovery beds on Floor 3, all pharmacy on Floor 4. Efficient for suppliers ('deliver 10 X-ray machines to Floor 1') but chaotic for a patient — every appointment means running between four floors.

- **Feature-first hospital:** the CARDIOLOGY WARD has ITS OWN X-ray, surgery room, recovery beds, pharmacy. So does the MATERNITY WARD. So does the ORTHOPEDIC WARD. Each ward is a **self-contained mini-hospital**. Adding a new specialty? Build a new ward without disturbing existing ones. Retire a specialty? Remove the ward.

**In code:**

- **Layer-first** organizes `lib/` by TECHNICAL TYPE: `lib/screens/`, `lib/models/`, `lib/services/`, `lib/widgets/`. All screens live together, all models together. Great for teaching Flutter; struggles at 100+ files.

- **Feature-first** organizes `lib/` by BUSINESS FEATURE: `lib/features/auth/`, `lib/features/cart/`, `lib/features/checkout/`. Each folder is self-contained — its own screens, models, services, widgets. To find something related to checkout, you look in ONE folder.

**Why this matters at scale:** in a 500-file codebase, layer-first means every task touches five folders. Feature-first means most tasks touch ONE folder. Cognitive load drops massively. **Feature-first is the recommended structure in Google's official Flutter architecture guide.**

**Feature-first architecture** organizes code by BUSINESS FEATURE rather than by TECHNICAL LAYER. `lib/features/auth/` contains everything auth-related — its screens, ViewModels, models, services, widgets. Contrast with **layer-first** where `lib/screens/`, `lib/models/`, `lib/services/` group across features. Feature-first scales better as apps grow: each feature is self-contained, discoverable, deletable. Google's Flutter architecture guide recommends this structure for anything beyond a demo app.
## Feature-first vs Layer-first — the directory tree

sections.code:
LAYER-FIRST (small apps, tutorials):                FEATURE-FIRST (production apps):

lib/                                              lib/
├── main.dart                                     ├── main.dart
├── screens/                                      ├── core/                    # shared infra
│   ├── login_screen.dart                         │   ├── router/
│   ├── home_screen.dart                          │   ├── theme/
│   ├── cart_screen.dart                          │   ├── services/           # AuthService, etc.
│   ├── checkout_screen.dart                      │   └── widgets/            # shared widgets
│   └── profile_screen.dart                       │
├── models/                                       ├── features/
│   ├── user.dart                                 │   ├── auth/               # self-contained
│   ├── product.dart                              │   │   ├── screens/
│   ├── order.dart                                │   │   ├── viewmodels/
│   └── cart.dart                                 │   │   ├── models/
├── services/                                     │   │   └── widgets/
│   ├── auth_service.dart                         │   ├── cart/
│   ├── api_service.dart                          │   │   ├── screens/
│   └── payment_service.dart                      │   │   ├── viewmodels/
├── widgets/                                      │   │   └── models/
│   ├── product_card.dart                         │   ├── checkout/
│   ├── cart_badge.dart                           │   └── profile/
│   └── loading_indicator.dart                    │
└── utils/                                        └── shared/                # cross-feature UI
                                                      └── product_card.dart

Problem at 100+ files:                            Benefit at 100+ files:
  ~ Finding checkout code = 5 folders               ~ Finding checkout code = 1 folder
  ~ Deleting a feature = surgical                   ~ Deleting a feature = rm -rf features/x
  ~ Reviewing PRs = jumping around                  ~ Reviewing PRs = one feature diff

sections.markdown: ## Feature-first + Clean Architecture — the combined structure

sections.code:
lib/
├── main.dart
├── core/
│   ├── router/
│   ├── theme/
│   ├── services/                       # shared services (Auth, Analytics, ...)
│   └── di.dart                         # composition root
│
├── features/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── domain/                     # Layer 1 + 2
│   │   │   ├── entities/
│   │   │   │   └── user.dart
│   │   │   ├── repositories/
│   │   │   │   └── auth_repository.dart      # interface
│   │   │   └── usecases/
│   │   │       └── sign_in.dart
│   │   ├── data/                       # Layer 3
│   │   │   ├── datasources/
│   │   │   ├── models/                 # DTOs
│   │   │   └── repositories/
│   │   │       └── auth_repository_impl.dart
│   │   └── presentation/               # Layer 4
│   │       ├── viewmodels/
│   │       ├── screens/
│   │       └── widgets/
│   │
│   ├── cart/
│   │   ├── domain/
│   │   ├── data/
│   │   └── presentation/
│   │
│   ├── checkout/
│   │   ├── domain/
│   │   ├── data/
│   │   └── presentation/
│   │
│   └── profile/
│       ├── domain/
│       ├── data/
│       └── presentation/
│
└── shared/                             # cross-feature reusable widgets, extensions
    ├── extensions/
    └── widgets/

# This is the STANDARD structure for a serious Flutter app.
# Google's Now In Android + Flutter reference apps use exactly this shape.

sections.markdown: ## Feature-first — three rules that keep it clean

rows: `features/cart/` never imports from `features/auth/`. Cross-feature communication goes via `core/services/`.
A widget used by 3+ features moves out of any single feature.
Feature exposes its route names + builders; router aggregates them.

sections.markdown: **The rule that matters most:** no cross-feature imports. If `features/cart/` imports from `features/auth/`, you've broken the encapsulation. Communication between features should happen via SHARED CORE (services, models in `shared/`, event streams). Otherwise deleting one feature affects the other.
## Sample program — a minimal feature-first `auth/` folder
## Common mistakes

**Mistake 1: Cross-feature imports.** `features/cart/viewmodels/cart_vm.dart` importing from `features/auth/entities/user.dart` — you've coupled them. Move `User` to `shared/` or `core/`, or communicate via a service.
**Mistake 2: Feature-first without layer discipline.** Everything in `features/foo/` in one file. Not much better than layer-first. Combine feature-first WITH Clean Architecture (nested layers).
**Mistake 3: God `core/`.** Everything ends up in `core/` because 'it might be shared'. Rule: something moves to `core/` only when 2+ features already use it.

**Mistake 4: One feature per screen.** `features/login_screen/`, `features/home_screen/`. That's just layer-first in disguise. A feature is a USER-VISIBLE CAPABILITY — auth (many screens), cart (many screens), profile (many screens).
**Mistake 5: Feature-first on a 3-screen app.** Overkill. Layer-first works fine for demos, tutorials, hackathons.
## Best practices
**1. Adopt feature-first at ~20+ screens.** Below that, layer-first is fine and less ceremonial.
**2. Combine with Clean Architecture.** `features/auth/{domain,data,presentation}/`.
**3. No cross-feature imports.** Enforce via `dart_code_metrics` or `analyzer_plugin`.
**4. Each feature owns its DI setup.** `registerAuthFeature()` in `features/auth/auth_di.dart`; called from the composition root.
**5. Shared widgets after they're used 3+ times.** Don't preemptively promote to `shared/`.
**6. Rule of THREE for extraction.** First use → in the feature. Second use → copy-paste. Third use → extract to `shared/`.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Convert layer-first to feature-first.** Take a small existing app; reorganize into `features/`.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Add Clean layers per feature.** `features/cart/{domain,data,presentation}/`.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Per-feature DI.** Each feature exposes a `register*Feature()` function.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Enforce no cross-feature imports.** Use `dart_code_metrics` or a custom lint rule.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Split a feature into a separate package.** `packages/cart/` as an independent pub package, versioned separately. This is modular architecture (see next topic).
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is feature-first architecture?**

**A:** Feature-first (aka 'vertical slicing') organizes code by BUSINESS FEATURE rather than by technical type. `lib/features/auth/` contains ALL auth-related code — its screens, ViewModels, models, services, widgets. Contrast: **layer-first** would put all screens in `lib/screens/`, all models in `lib/models/`, all services in `lib/services/`. **Why:** at 100+ files, layer-first requires jumping across five folders for any task; feature-first keeps most tasks in one folder. Cognitive load drops. Google's official Flutter architecture guide recommends feature-first for anything beyond a demo app.

**Q2: Feature-first vs layer-first — when to use which?**

**A:** **Layer-first** — great for tutorials, small demos, personal projects, apps under ~20 screens. Simple, easy to teach. **Feature-first** — the recommended structure for production apps and anything at scale. Better discoverability, easier PR reviews (one feature = one diff), simpler onboarding (developers work in one folder). **My rule:** start layer-first for a 2-week MVP; migrate to feature-first when you cross ~20 screens or add a second team. The migration is straightforward — files move; imports fix themselves; the underlying code doesn't change. Don't over-engineer at day 1; DO plan the migration.

**Q3: How does feature-first combine with Clean Architecture?**

**A:** Feature-first is HORIZONTAL organization (by business capability); Clean Architecture is VERTICAL organization (by dependency layer). They compose beautifully: `lib/features/auth/{domain,data,presentation}/`. Each feature gets its own three layers. The **`core/`** or **`shared/`** folder holds cross-cutting infrastructure (services, router, theme, DI setup, shared widgets). This is Google's Now In Android structure and the recommended Flutter architecture. Result: each feature is self-contained, testable, deletable, with clear internal layering.

**Q4: What's the 'no cross-feature imports' rule?**

**A:** Files inside `features/cart/` should NEVER import from `features/auth/`. If they do, you've coupled the features — deleting one affects the other; testing requires setting up both; refactoring cascades. **How features communicate:** (1) Shared models/services in `core/` or `shared/`. `AuthService.currentUser` is called from anywhere. (2) Shared route table in `core/router/`. (3) Event streams for cross-feature notifications. **Enforce via lint rules** (`dart_code_metrics`, custom analyzer plugins) — otherwise the boundary erodes silently. This is the rule that keeps feature-first useful at scale.

**Q5: What goes in `core/` vs a feature vs `shared/`?**

**A:** **`core/`** — INFRASTRUCTURE required by many features: router, theme, DI setup, singleton services (AuthService, AnalyticsService), app-wide constants. **`features/`** — one folder per business capability. Own screens, VMs, models, services (feature-specific), widgets. **`shared/`** — reusable UI widgets and extensions used by MULTIPLE features. `ProductCard` used in cart, wishlist, and search → belongs here. **Rule of thumb:** something goes in `shared/` only after being copy-pasted twice — the third use case is when you extract. Preemptive `shared/` bloats it into another `core/`.

**Q6: How do you handle a screen that spans multiple features?**

**A:** Cross-feature screens are one of the hardest calls. Options: **(1) Host in the 'primary' feature.** A `checkout` screen that shows cart items and captures payment lives in `features/checkout/`; it consumes `features/cart/` state via a shared service. **(2) Create a new feature that composes.** `features/checkout/` composes cart + payment domain concepts. **(3) Put in `core/` if truly system-level** (e.g., a global error screen). **Anti-pattern:** the screen imports from three feature folders directly. **Rule:** cross-feature screens compose via SERVICES and SHARED MODELS, not via cross-feature file imports.

**Q7: What are common feature-first mistakes?**

**A:** **(1) Cross-feature imports** — the #1 discipline erosion. Enforce with lint. **(2) Everything in `core/`** — 'it might be shared' — the folder becomes a dumping ground. Rule: promote to `core/` when 2+ features already use it. **(3) One feature per screen** — `features/login_screen/`, `features/home_screen/`. That's layer-first pretending. A feature is a USER-VISIBLE CAPABILITY, not a UI file. **(4) Feature-first without layers** — the feature folder becomes a soup of files. Combine feature-first + Clean Architecture. **(5) Adopting feature-first on a 3-screen app** — overkill; layer-first is fine at that scale. **(6) Not per-feature DI** — a single global `setupDI()` that knows every feature; scales poorly. Each feature should register its own dependencies.

## Real-world usage

- **Google's Now In Android** — canonical feature-first + Clean Architecture reference (Kotlin/Compose).
- **Google's Flutter samples 2024+** — feature-first structure.
- **Enterprise apps at 100+ screens** — nearly universally feature-first.
- **Multi-team apps** — each team owns one or more features.
- **Not for tutorials, demos, or hackathons** — layer-first is simpler.
- **Feature-first with a monorepo (melos)** — features can become separate packages (see modular architecture).

## Advanced notes

- **Feature toggles** — features can be dropped in/out via a route table.
- **Package-per-feature** — feature folder becomes its own `pub` package for strict isolation (see modular).
- **`melos` for monorepos** — manages many pub packages in one repo.
- **Feature ownership** — each feature has an OWNERS file + code-review assignment.
- **Feature module tests** — each feature has its own `test/` folder; runs in isolation.
- **Golden tests per feature** — widget snapshots inside the feature's folder.
- **Codeowners in GitHub/GitLab** — auto-request review from feature owners.
- **Refactoring across features** — expensive; sometimes signals a missing shared abstraction.
- **Slice tests** — each feature has one integration test that exercises its own happy path.
- **Deprecation** — a feature can be marked deprecated by adding a `deprecated.md` in its root; auto-removed in a later release.

flashcards.back: Organize lib/ by BUSINESS FEATURE (features/auth/, features/cart/) — each self-contained.
Organize lib/ by TECHNICAL TYPE (screens/, models/, services/). Fine for small apps.
Around 20+ screens. Below that, layer-first works.
No cross-feature imports. Communicate via core/services or shared models.
After 3+ features use the thing — not before.

options: Only after 3+ features use the thing

challenges.text: Convert a 5-screen layer-first project to feature-first.
Each feature has its own register*Feature() DI function.
Enforce no cross-feature imports with dart_code_metrics.
Extract a common ProductCard widget to shared/ after 3 uses.
Add a top-level router that aggregates per-feature routes.
Split one feature into its own pub package (see modular).

How does feature-first combine with Clean Architecture?
What goes in core/ vs a feature vs shared/?
How do you handle a screen that spans multiple features?
